Messages still arrive — they just only notice them once they actually open Teams. Their phones are unaffected. Windows updates ran last week and at least one person had some focus mode turned on during a meeting.

Is this connected or just multiple incidents at once? And please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Summary - You are seeing four separate symptom groups that match previously recorded, distinct incidents in the knowledge base. The records show different root causes and different remediation owners/levels for each. I list for each issue: (A) what the records explicitly document as symptoms, diagnostics and fixes; and (B) the concrete step sequence you should follow based only on those documented cases. After that I state what the records do not contain (where further investigation is required) and give the ticket-level classification implied by the prior incidents. 1) D365 Finance & Operations - Financial Reports unusable (spin / error / authors "unknown" / "report cannot be found") A. What the records explicitly document - Identical symptom set was reported in ticket TI-722511: Financial Reports -> Edit errors or endless spin, creators shown as "unknown", "report cannot be found", reproducible across users and environments; network and ADFS initially ruled out [Data: Sources (0, 1)]. - Documented diagnostics performed: Data Mart Reset and recreation of FRDB in the D365 environment; Microsoft support ticket was opened when initial steps did not resolve [Data: Sources (1)]. - Final remediation recorded in the closure text: best-practice cipher suites were activated using IISCrypto and AOS servers were restarted; ticket closed after that [Data: Sources (0)]. B. Step‑by‑step resolution (drawn from the records) 1. Reproduce the problem (multiple users, multiple clients / browsers) and confirm the same symptoms (spinning, "unknown" authors, "report cannot be found") - as documented in TI-722511 [Data: Sources (0, 1)]. 2. Perform a Data Mart Reset and, if applicable, recreate the FR database (FRDB) in the affected environment(s) - these steps were executed during the incident investigation [Data: Sources (1)]. 3. If the issue persists, open a Microsoft support ticket (the prior case escalated to Microsoft) - a Microsoft ticket was created in that incident [Data: Sources (1)]. 4. Apply best-practice cipher suites via IISCrypto and restart AOS servers - this action is recorded as the closure remediation that resolved the incident [Data: Sources (0)]. C. Gaps / additional notes - The records show these exact steps were performed and that the IISCrypto + AOS restart resolved the prior incident. The documentation does not include command-level details or the exact cipher list used; those specifics are not present in the indexed records. If you require the exact cipher configuration used previously, open the Microsoft ticket or check the change record that applied IISCrypto (not included in the indexed texts) - this is a recommended next step, not documented in the records. 2) New hotline numbers "dead" (carrier says edge receives calls but calls never reach users) A. What the records explicitly document - A very similar symptom (new numbers not reaching users though carrier indicates delivery to the edge) occurred in ticket TI-1609823 / TI-1612036: calls from certain ranges reached the SBC but the SBC did not forward them internally (SBC returned errors such as 404 or the translation/routing table lacked the new range) [Data: Sources (9, 8)]. - Remediation in those incidents: adjust SBC translation and routing rules to include the new number range (temporary rule change proved the hypothesis; permanent update implemented) [Data: Sources (9, 8)]. B. Step‑by‑step resolution (drawn from the records) 1. Collect example failing numbers and timestamps; ask the carrier for a call trace showing SIP INVITE arrival at your edge (this was the starting point in the prior incidents) [Data: Sources (9, 8)]. 2. Check SBC signalling logs for the failing calls (look for INVITE handling and any immediate 4xx/5xx responses) - prior cases found INVITE accepted for working numbers but INVITE answered with "404 Not Found" for affected ranges [Data: Sources (9)]. 3. Inspect the SBC translation/normalization table and voice routing policies to verify the new number range is present. In prior incidents the new range was missing from the SBC translation table / routing rules [Data: Sources (9, 8)]. 4. Temporarily add a translation/routing rule for the new number range and perform test calls (the temporary change was used to validate the fix in the record) [Data: Sources (9)]. 5. After successful tests, apply the permanent update to the translation/routing configuration and verify across affected numbers [Data: Sources (9, 8)]. C. Gaps / additional notes - The records confirm the resolution approach (SBC translation/routing update) but do not contain the exact translation patterns or SBC configuration snippets for your environment. Those details must be determined from your SBC configuration and routing blueprint; they are not in the indexed records. This is a documented remediation approach but the implementation specifics are environment‑specific (recommended action: a 2nd‑level Telephony/System Engineer with SBC access should perform the steps). 3) Basel office - external calls drop after ~30 seconds A. What the records explicitly document - Ticket TI-1604879 describes identical behaviour: external calls disconnect after ~30 seconds at the Basel location, internal calls fine; a new Voice VLAN and a firewall/NAT policy change had been implemented recently [Data: Sources (16)]. - Diagnostics found Session-Expires header set to 30 seconds and NAT/UDP session timeout on firewall configured to 30 seconds. SBC showed a BYE at ~30s. Temporary increasing of session timers/SBC parameters fixed the test calls; final remediation was to adjust NAT session timeout to match architecture (e.g., 180s) and adjust SBC session parameters [Data: Sources (16)]. B. Step‑by‑step resolution (drawn from the records) 1. Reproduce a failing external call from Basel and capture SIP and SBC logs to see timing and any BYE messages - prior diagnostics captured BYE from the SBC after ~30s [Data: Sources (16)]. 2. Inspect the Session-Expires header and SBC session timers; compare to other locations where calls are stable (other locations used 180s in the record) [Data: Sources (16)]. 3. Check firewall/NAT UDP session timeout for the new Voice VLAN (Basel had NAT session timeout = 30s, Zurich = 180s in the record) [Data: Sources (16)]. 4. Temporarily adjust session parameters on the SBC (as test) - prior temporary SBC change removed the premature drops during testing [Data: Sources (16)]. 5. Permanently adjust the firewall/NAT UDP session timeout to the value required by the Direct Routing architecture and align SBC session timer settings accordingly; re-test calls [Data: Sources (16)]. 6. Monitor calls for regression; prior ticket monitored and confirmed stability after the adjustments [Data: Sources (16)]. C. Gaps / additional notes - The records clearly document the cause and fix. They do not contain the exact firewall GUI/CLI commands you must run in your environment; those are environment-specific and not present in the indexed records. A 2nd‑level Network / UC Engineer should perform the change (this escalation path is consistent with the documented incident). 4) Teams - laptop pop‑ups/notifications not shown (phones OK) A. What the records explicitly document - Ticket TI-1615096 describes identical symptoms: messages arrive but no desktop pop-ups; phones unaffected; Windows Update ran recently; Focus Assist was active on the laptop. The root cause in that case was Teams banner notifications disabled and Windows Focus Assist blocking notifications. Helpdesk followed a "Blueprint - Teams Notifications Standard" checklist and re-enabled the notification channel and disabled Focus Assist; notifications returned [Data: Sources (19)]. B. Step‑by‑step resolution (drawn from the records) 1. Confirm affected device(s) and whether issue occurs only on laptop(s) (in the prior case only the laptop was affected) [Data: Sources (19)]. 2. On the laptop, check Teams notification setting for banner (banner disabled was found in the record) and re-enable it if disabled [Data: Sources (19)]. 3. Check Windows Notification Center / Focus Assist settings and disable Focus Assist or set appropriate automatic rules to allow Teams pop-ups - Focus Assist was blocking pop-ups in the incident [Data: Sources (19)]. 4. Restart Teams and verify notifications reappear; these steps were followed as per the Blueprint in the recorded case [Data: Sources (19)]. 5. If required, follow the documented Blueprint checks (Teams settings, Windows Notification Center, Focus Assist, Teams restart) - all were performed in the prior incident and fixed it [Data: Sources (19)]. C. Gaps / additional notes - The records contain the complete troubleshooting checklist and resolution for this symptom; Helpdesk (1st level) handled it in the prior case. If after these steps notifications remain blocked, escalate to 2nd level with device logs. Are these connected or separate incidents? - Based on the records, these are separate, unrelated root causes: - D365 Financial Reports: application / AOS / cipher-suite / Data Mart issue and required AOS/IISCrypto changes and Microsoft escalation in past case [Data: Sources (0, 1)]. - Hotline numbers: telephony routing / SBC translation issue that required updating translation/routing tables [Data: Sources (9, 8)]. - Basel call drops: NAT / session timer mismatch between firewall and SBC after a VLAN change [Data: Sources (16)]. - Teams notifications: desktop client / OS settings (Teams banner + Focus Assist) - resolved at Helpdesk level [Data: Sources (19)]. - The records treat them as separate incidents and they were handled by different teams/owners in prior cases [Data: Sources (0, 1, 9, 16, 19)]. Classification: 1st‑level or 2nd‑level? - Evidence from the indexed incidents: - Teams desktop notifications were resolved by Helpdesk (1st level) using a standard blueprint [Data: Sources (19)] - classify as 1st level. - D365 Financial Reports required system engineering actions, Data Mart reset, and escalation to Microsoft and changes to AOS/IIS settings - this aligns with 2nd level / application infrastructure and vendor escalation [Data: Sources (1, 0)]. - Hotline numbers (new range not routed) required SBC translation/routing changes and telephony System Engineer / Direct Routing architecture actions - classify as 2nd level (telephony/voice engineering) [Data: Sources (9, 8)]. - Basel session timeout required SBC and network/firewall changes (2nd level: UC/Network) [Data: Sources (16)]. Suggested immediate triage (practical, and consistent with prior tickets) - Open four separate tickets or sub‑tasks assigned to the correct teams, because prior records show different owners and escalation paths: 1. D365 Financial Reports -> assign to System Engineer / D365 infra and, if not solved by Data Mart Reset, escalate to Microsoft (TI-722511 pattern) [Data: Sources (1, 0)]. 2. Hotline numbers -> assign to Telephony / SBC owner (check SBC logs and translation tables; temporary rule test; then permanent update) [Data: Sources (9, 8)]. 3. Basel call drops -> assign to Network + UC (inspect Session-Expires, SBC timers, firewall/NAT UDP session timeout; align timers) [Data: Sources (16)]. 4. Teams notifications -> handle in Helpdesk (guide user to verify Teams banner + disable Focus Assist; follow the "Blueprint - Teams Notifications Standard") [Data: Sources (19)]. What the records do NOT provide (so you still need to collect these) - Exact cipher suite list and IISCrypto configuration used in TI-722511 closure (the record states IISCRYPTO was used but does not list the ciphers) - not present in the indexed data [Data: Sources (0)]. - The exact SBC translation/normalization entries and the specific routing table lines required for your new hotline number ranges - prior tickets record the fix approach but not your environment's exact config [Data: Sources (9, 8)]. - Firewall/NAT CLI/GUI commands or the exact session timeout values to set for your specific firewall model - the prior case documents the symptom and that the timeout was adjusted, but not vendor-specific commands [Data: Sources (16)]. If you want, I can: - Produce the precise ordered checklist for the technicians to run (mapped to the appropriate teams) based strictly on the steps above and attach the relevant ticket references for each checklist item; or - Draft the four ticket templates with the exact diagnostic artifacts to collect (SIP traces, SBC logs, AOS logs, screenshots of Teams notification settings, etc.) so you can assign them to the correct groups. If you want me to prepare either of those, tell me which one and I will produce it (again, strictly using the procedures and examples documented in the indexed records). References - D365 Financial Reports incident and remediation (Data Mart Reset, FRDB recreate, Microsoft ticket, IISCrypto + AOS restart): TI-722511 [Data: Sources (0, 1)]. - Hotline / new number routing issues and SBC translation/routing fix: TI-1609823 / related Direct Routing case [Data: Sources (9, 8)]. - Basel external calls dropping after ~30s - NAT/session timeout and SBC session timer fix: TI-1604879 [Data: Sources (16)]. - Teams notifications blocked by disabled banner + Windows Focus Assist - Helpdesk Blueprint resolution: TI-1615096 [Data: Sources (19)]. If you want me to turn the above into four ready-to-assign tickets (including which logs/traces to collect and example phrasing), say which format you prefer.

question_text:
Hi IT, we are a couple of weeks into our migration from our old internal sign-in system to the new cloud-based one. The first batch of users went through smoothly, but since we did the second batch about two weeks ago, we have been getting a series of strange complaints from those users that we cannot make sense of.

First, several users from the second batch say they can sign in to their cloud email and Teams without any problem, but when they try to open our internal procurement system or our document management system (which run on our internal servers), they get an error saying their account is not recognized. The first-batch users do not have this issue.

Second, we are seeing strange inconsistencies with security groups. A user might be correctly in the new combined group on the internal directory side, but in the cloud they still show as a member of an old group. This is breaking some access permissions because the cloud and internal systems read different group memberships for the same person.

Third, the same affected users are saying their mobile email apps (built-in iPhone Mail and the Android Gmail app) keep asking for password and never connect. Outlook on the phone still works fine for them but the built-in mail apps just loop on the password prompt.

Fourth — and this only came up because someone noticed overnight reports were missing — several scheduled tasks that run on our internal servers using service accounts have started failing with authentication errors. The service accounts have not been touched by the migration as far as we know, but the failures started right around the time the second batch was migrated.

These all started in the same window but they look like four completely different problems. We need to figure out what is going on and how to fix it.
answer: Summary - what the indexed records show (explicitly documented) - The four symptoms you describe match a known set of incidents during KF70's second migration wave: (a) cloud sign-in works but on‑prem apps fail because on‑prem UPNs were overwritten to a new suffix; (b) cloud group membership remained the old groups because the new groups were placed outside the AAD Connect OU filter; (c) built‑in mobile mail clients failed because tenant Modern Authentication was enforced and those clients tried Basic Auth; (d) scheduled tasks using service accounts failed because those accounts were included in the tenant Conditional Access (MFA) target group and were synced/licensed unexpectedly. These root causes and their fixes are documented in tickets TI-1700001, TI-1700002, TI-1700003 and TI-1700004 [Data: Sources (22, 53, 4, 49)]. Step‑by‑step resolution based on the documented fixes Below are the concrete steps that were performed in the recorded incidents. If you want us to perform these for your environment we can follow the same sequence; where the records show actions taken I cite the ticket. 1) Fix "can sign into M365 but not on‑prem apps" (UPN suffix mismatch) - Verify whether affected users' on‑prem userPrincipalName was changed to the new cloud suffix (kf70group.local) while on‑prem apps still expect kf70.local - this mismatch was the documented root cause [Data: Sources (22)]. - If UPNs were overwritten, revert the on‑prem userPrincipalName values to the original kf70.local form for affected accounts (the record shows this was done for 25 users) [Data: Sources (22)]. - Trigger a full Azure AD Connect synchronization after the reversion to ensure on‑prem and cloud attributes align [Data: Sources (22)]. - Validate by testing 2-3 affected users against the on‑prem applications (the ticket validation step used 3 users before wider validation) [Data: Sources (22)]. 2) Fix cloud/on‑prem group membership divergence (AAD Connect OU filter) - Confirm whether the new group objects for the second wave were created in an OU outside the Azure AD Connect sync scope (the documented OU name was Migration_Phase2) [Data: Sources (53, 54)]. - Update the Azure AD Connect OU filter to include the OU containing the new group structure (the engineers added Migration_Phase2 to the sync scope in the record) [Data: Sources (53, 54)]. - Run an initial delta then a full sync to bring group objects and memberships into Entra ID and propagate member relationships [Data: Sources (53, 54)]. - Validate that affected users now show the intended group memberships in Microsoft 365 and that downstream access (SharePoint, apps) evaluates correctly [Data: Sources (53, 54)]. 3) Fix mobile built‑in mail clients failing (Modern Auth / EAS Basic Auth blocked) - Confirm that Modern Authentication is enforced tenant‑wide (this was enabled as part of the migration baseline) and that affected clients (iOS built‑in Mail, Android Gmail using EAS) are attempting Basic Auth [Data: Sources (4)]. - For affected users, remove the old cached credentials on the device and re‑add the account using a client/method that supports OAuth (Outlook mobile or updated built‑in client with modern auth). In the record they instructed customers to update the mobile clients to use modern auth and to re‑enroll affected devices in Intune where appropriate [Data: Sources (4)]. - Validate by confirming those devices can connect and that Outlook mobile continues to work for any users who use it [Data: Sources (4)]. 4) Fix scheduled tasks failing with authentication errors (service accounts & Conditional Access) - Check whether service accounts were moved into the tenant target group (KF70-AllUsers-Hybrid) that the baseline Conditional Access policy KF70-Require-MFA targets; sign‑in logs were used to confirm "Failed - Conditional Access requires MFA" in the documented case [Data: Sources (49, 50)]. - Create a dedicated exclusion group for service accounts (the record shows KF70-ServiceAccounts-NoMFA was created), add the service accounts to it, and add that group as an explicit exclusion to the Conditional Access policy so MFA is not applied to non‑interactive logons [Data: Sources (49, 50)]. - Re‑evaluate the Azure AD Connect filter to avoid unintentionally syncing service accounts (which led to license assignment and subtle auth path changes in the documented case) [Data: Sources (49, 50)]. - Validate by confirming scheduled tasks run successfully overnight (the customer confirmed success in the ticket) [Data: Sources (49, 50)]. Validation and documentation steps that were performed in the incidents - After reverting UPNs the engineers triggered a full sync and validated a sample of users (3 users) before wider validation, then confirmed all 25 users could sign in to both cloud and on‑prem apps [Data: Sources (22)]. - After updating OU filters they ran delta/full syncs and the customer validated group memberships and SharePoint permissions [Data: Sources (53, 54)]. - After changing CA exclusions and AAD Connect scope, scheduled tasks ran successfully and the migration runbook was updated to require explicit service‑account handling [Data: Sources (49, 50)]. - Mobile client fix required user device action (remove/re‑add account, re‑enrol) and confirmation that Outlook mobile continued to work [Data: Sources (4)]. What the indexed records do not contain / additional information we need from you (recommended) The prior cases show that similar fixes worked, but to apply them to your environment we need the same basic diagnostic inputs that were requested in those tickets. The records show the engineers asked for these items before acting - please provide them so we can proceed safely: - 2-3 sample affected user UPNs and the exact error messages returned by the on‑prem applications (Employee 30 requested sample UPNs in TI‑1700001) [Data: Sources (22)]. Recommended. - Names of the affected groups and the OU where the new group objects were created (TI‑1700002 used UserA/UserB and Migration_Phase2 as examples) [Data: Sources (53, 54)]. Recommended. - A list of the service accounts used by scheduled tasks (for example svc‑kf70‑rpt, svc‑kf70‑bkp01, svc‑kf70‑monitor were provided in TI‑1700004) so we can check their group membership, CA evaluation, and sync status [Data: Sources (49)]. Recommended. - Mobile device details (OS versions, whether the built‑in client or Outlook mobile is in use) so we can determine whether a client update or re‑enrolment is required (TI‑1700003 recorded that affected clients were built‑in iOS/Android and that Outlook mobile worked) [Data: Sources (4)]. Recommended. - If you can, provide Azure AD Connect configuration export (current OU filter), a sample of affected user on‑prem attributes (userPrincipalName), and the applicable Conditional Access policy details - these were used in the documented investigations and are required to make the same changes safely [Data: Sources (22, 53, 49)]. If you want, I can: - Apply the same sequence of fixes documented above (revert UPNs where overwritten -> full sync; update OU filter -> full sync; create service‑account exclusion and adjust CA; instruct users/devices to move to modern auth), or - Produce a runbook of the exact commands and UI steps to execute these changes in your environment (note: the indexed records describe what was done but do not contain verbatim command lists; any specific commands would be produced as a recommended next step and are not verbatim in the records). If you want me to proceed with the documented fixes, provide the diagnostic items listed above and I will follow the documented steps and report results back.

question_text:
Hi IT, we need help asap because it feels like the whole company is falling apart since the weekend changes. I'm trying to describe it without guessing, but it's a mess.

1) In D365 (F&O) the Financial Reports / Report Designer thing is basically broken. It either loads forever or errors out. What's super strange: older reports suddenly show the author as 'unknown' and sometimes it says a report 'cannot be found' even when we used it last week. This happens in both test and prod, so we can't even use test as a workaround.

2) Sales: the desktop mail app is acting up. People can receive emails, but anything they send just sits in Outbox and later fails with a connection-style error. If they send the same email from the browser mailbox it goes through. Mobile seems fine too.

3) External partners: in Teams they are shown as Offline all day even while they are actively writing to us. Chats arrive late and sometimes calls to them do not ring. Internals are fine.

4) Zurich site: external calls in Teams connect, the other side says they hear us, but on our side it's dead silent (zero audio). We swapped headsets and speakers and it's the same. Internal calls are ok.

5) Basel site: external calls connect and then drop after about half a minute almost every time, like a timer. Internal calls are ok.

6) Also with the new marketing hotline numbers: calls to the old main number work, but calls to the new number range fail instantly. The provider claims they deliver the call to our edge.

7) Printing is chaos too, but it's not one single thing. A new employee in Basel sees literally no printers at all, even though everyone next to them can print. Another user can see the printer but gets 'Access denied' immediately when printing. And for the rest of us: jobs show up in the queue and then just sit there forever like nothing is processing.

8) One more: in Basel the main office printer sometimes shows jobs for a second and then they vanish, while other printers in the building still work.

People keep blaming laptops, docks, and Windows updates, but all of this started at basically the same time after the weekend work. Is this one connected change or just unlucky timing? And please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Summary of what's documented in our records vs what is not, and a step‑by‑step resolution path using only documented fixes and diagnostics. Important: below I separate (A) actions and root causes that the indexed tickets actually document, and (B) recommended next steps where the indexed knowledge does not contain a complete resolution. I cite the source ticket(s) for each documented item. 1) D365 F&O - Financial Reports / Report Designer (loads forever, authors shown as "unknown", "report cannot be found") - What the records explicitly document: A nearly identical incident was logged as KF11: Management Report not working (TI-722511). The problem occurred reproducibly in all environments, affected different users and machines, and showed slow Report Designer performance and intermittent errors. The documented diagnostics and remediation steps performed were: Data Mart Reset and recreation of the FRDB in the integration environment; opening a Microsoft support ticket (#2404050050000982); and later applying IISCrypto best-practice cipher suites and restarting AOS servers. The ticket shows the Data Mart reset and FRDB recreation were performed, the Microsoft ticket was opened, and ultimately IISCrypto changes and AOS restarts were applied [Data: Sources (0, 1)]. - What that implies for your case (documented actions you can follow or request): - Documented fixes to try/confirm (from TI-722511): perform Data Mart Reset and recreate FRDB if applicable; engage Microsoft support if Data Mart / FRDB work does not fix it; apply best-practice cipher suites via IISCRYPTO and restart AOS servers (this was the closure action in the record) [Data: Sources (0, 1)]. - Recommended (not directly documented for your environment): if you've had weekend change(s) to AOS, IIS, or TLS/cipher configuration, request an AOS restart and review recent IIS/IISCrypto changes first. (This recommendation is outside the indexed records - please treat it as suggested next steps.) 2) Desktop Outlook - emails stuck in Outbox, web/mobile OK - What the records explicitly document: A matching incident (KF22 / TI-1548823) showed Outlook clients could receive but not send; OWA and mobile worked. Root cause there was SMTP AUTH being disabled by tenant Security Defaults and affected clients were legacy Outlook builds. The documented remediation was selectively re‑enabling SMTP AUTH for affected users and adjusting Conditional Access policies; after that sending worked and mailflow was monitored [Data: Sources (32)]. - What that implies for your case (documented actions you can request): Verify whether SMTP AUTH was disabled by a security policy change (Security Defaults/Conditional Access) and, if so, apply the documented remediation pattern from TI-1548823: selectively enable SMTP AUTH for impacted accounts and adjust Conditional Access to allow legacy clients, then test sending [Data: Sources (32)]. - Recommended (not in records): If you prefer not to re-enable SMTP AUTH, plan client upgrades (modern authentication) - this is a general recommendation, not documented as a performed remediation in the indexed tickets. 3) Teams presence & federation - external partners shown Offline, delayed chats, calls not ringing - What the records explicitly document: I do not find any record in the indexed tickets that documents exactly this presence/federation symptom (external contacts shown Offline while active, delayed chat arrival, external calls failing to ring). There are Teams call/connectivity tickets in the dataset, but none that cover presence/federation state for external partners [No supporting data in indexed records]. - Recommended (not documented): escalate to a 2nd‑level/Teams/Identity engineer to check federation/trust, tenant settings, and conditional access affecting presence and presence publication. Also collect logs (client presence traces, federation status, Teams service health) before/after the weekend change.
